title: Qlog Contained CBOR Serialization Architecture
purpose: Describe how the first qlog CBOR serializer is added as a sibling package over the retained qlog model without forcing unstable sink concerns back into the current public surface.
design_summary: Add a contained-only CBOR writer boundary in a sibling `Incursa.Qlog.Cbor` package so the repository gains a first binary format without reshaping the core JSON serializer contracts or the still-internal capture pipeline. The architecture preserves the same file, trace, event, schema-URI, and extension semantics already owned by the main qlog model, exposes stream-first byte output, reuses the contained qlog file schema URI, records `application/cbor` as the serialization_format media type, and uses `.qlog.cbor` as the canonical file extension.
key_components: retained qlog file and trace model reuse
data_and_state_considerations: The CBOR slice reuses the same contained qlog file semantics as the JSON writer rather than introducing a second domain model. File-envelope metadata, trace metadata, event payloads, schema URIs, common fields, and unknown extension members all need to survive binary serialization without collapsing into JSON-specific assumptions. The contained file schema URI remains `urn:ietf:params:qlog:file:contained` because file schema and serialization format are separate concerns in the qlog model. The first architecture deliberately avoids a sequential or append-oriented CBOR stream contract. Built-in sinks remain a separate concern even after the serializer package and artifact metadata policy are closed.
edge_cases_and_constraints: The first CBOR slice should preserve private and extension schema URIs as opaque identifiers.
The upstream draft does not currently define a qlog-specific CBOR media type or file-extension policy, so the repository uses `application/cbor` and `.qlog.cbor` as the first local policy.
Field ordering should not become a cross-format semantic requirement unless the CBOR mapping explicitly defines one.
The first CBOR slice should not widen the current non-public capture or sink contracts.
alternatives_considered: Transcoding through the JSON serializer instead of writing CBOR from the retained model.
Introducing a broad multi-format abstraction before the first binary format exists.
Bundling the first CBOR serializer directly into the built-in sink lane.
Deferring CBOR entirely until a future protocol vocabulary forces it.
risks: The first local media-type and file-extension policy could still diverge from a future upstream standard.
Binary-format-specific shortcuts could diverge from the contained JSON semantics that the retained model already owns.
Mixing CBOR planning into the sink lane too early could blur the boundary between serializer and publication behavior.
